data.title: Main Street Capital's Valuation Problem: How Interest Rates Could Derail Its Dividend Appeal
data.description:
I have covered Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) before, where I outlined the investment thesis in detail, and in my last article, explained why I considered it overvalued. Since then, the stock reached my fair value, but after the recent increase, and lower NAV appreciation than I expected, I still consider it overvalued.

Background

Main Street Capital Corporation is a US business development company that provides capital, through debt and equity, to small and mid-sized US businesses.

MAIN divides its investments into four categories: Lower Middle Market (LMM), Private Loan, Middle Market, and other; however, the first two categories represent almost all of its business, accounting for 91% of the investments. Specifically, the LMM segment accounts for approximately 54% of the total investment, while Private Loan accounts for about 37%.

The Lower Middle Market targets companies with annual revenue between $10 million and $150 million and EBITDA between $3 million and $20 million and offers debt and equity financing.

The Private Loan segment consists of debt investments in privately held companies typically owned or being acquired by a private equity fund and targets bigger companies with annual revenue between $25 million and $500 million and EBITDA between $7.5-$50 million.

While most of MAIN’s invested capital is directed to recapitalization or refinancing of companies, in general, the company is very well diversified with investments in 185 different companies, and the largest one represents less than 5% of the total portfolio.

The industry diversification is also great, with no sector with a weight higher than 10%.

MAIN’s main exposure is its interest rate sensitivity. While 75% of the LMM investments are at fixed rates, the Private Loan segment is the opposite, with 96% of investments being at floating rates. This could represent a problem because if rates go down, MAIN's supplemental dividend could be impacted.

In general, 65% of MAIN’s debt investments are at floating rates, while 90% of MAIN’s outstanding debt is at fixed rates. This means that the impact of lower rates is much higher than the benefit for the debt payments.

Moreover, as I will comment in the next section in more detail, MAIN’s cheapest debt matures next year, and new issuance will be more expensive.

This is important, because MAIN’s principal attraction is its monthly dividend and its special dividends.

While I believe that the company will continue to increase its monthly dividend, if rates come down as Trump intends, the special dividend could be frozen or even reduced.

Financial Position and Valuation

MAIN has a strong financial position with $30 million in cash, $2.1 billion in debt, and $4.9 billion in investments. While cash may seem low, the company has access to credit facilities in case it needs to do new investments.

More concerning is that nearly a quarter of its debt matures this July. While I don’t expect MAIN to have any problem issuing new debt, the debt maturing is its cheapest, at a 3% fixed rate. In my last article, I suggested that MAIN could issue debt at a 6% rate. In August the company issued $350 million at 5.4%; however, this lower coupon is because the maturity was just three years out, while I was expecting closer to five. I believe that the company shortened the duration to pay less than its credit facility.

I expect MAIN to use the credit facility, with a cost around 6%, to pay the July maturity and issue debt with short maturities to try to reduce its interest expenses. However, refinancing nearly a quarter of its debt at double the interest expense will weigh on the company’s earnings.

The combination of interest rate headwinds and historically elevated valuations makes me cautious about the stock.

Assuming that it trades back to its last few years mean valuation, the stock's fair value should be $54 per share; however, given the interest rate headwinds, I believe the valuation could decline further.

Risks

I believe that there are two main risks. The first is that valuation continues to increase and the stock trades above 2x NAV. While 2x NAV has been the ceiling so far, there is no assurance that the stock won’t trade higher if sentiment remains strong.

The other risk is that interest rates move higher. In that case, MAIN will benefit, as most of its debt is fixed while its investments have more exposure to floating rates. In that case, the company could continue to increase its monthly dividend and the special dividend, attracting more investors.

Conclusion

MAIN valuation remains at elevated levels, while possible headwinds could halt the dividend increase or, in more extreme scenarios, reduce the special dividend.

When the 2026 notes mature, interest expenses will increase considerably, weighing on the company’s earnings.

Taking that into consideration and assuming its average NAV multiple since 2021, my fair value is $54 per share; however, I consider that there could be more downside.

data.title: NRG Energy: Positioned To Monetize The AI Power Boom
data.description:
Why NRG Sits at the Center of the Grid Transformation

Electricity in the U.S. is a hot topic that keeps intensifying month after month. We are talking about record levels after record levels, as after the estimated 4,195 TWh for 2025, 4,268 TWh are projected for 2026 and 4,372 TWh for 2027, a situation that has clearly become structural.

We can find clues as to what is driving this situation; for example, in Texas, ERCOT has been managing a “large load” request queue of around 205 GW versus a peak demand that was considered historic at around 85 GW.

Basically, this means that just the pipeline of interconnection requests is already roughly equivalent to 2x the maximum load the system has needed on a day of very high intensity.

In PJM, I see a similar tone, as the operator’s plan to manage this surge in demand is that either new consumers, i.e., data centers, start bringing their own generation, or they will have to enter into a scheme where they accept curtailment in case of system stress.

To me, these are figures that practically compete with the total installed capacity of entire countries, which fits perfectly with the upward revisions in CapEx that we are seeing, as well as with grid planning that can support the new environment.

And this is where NRG Energy, Inc. (NYSE:NRG) comes in. That is why I decided to write about it, because in this new scenario it is a piece that fits in a fairly natural way. The business model combines retail, generation, and demand management, but with the closing of the LS Power deal, NRG adds 13 GW of gas generation, practically doubling its capacity to around 25 GW. This scale has led to an update of its guidance for 2026 to a range of $5.32B to $5.82B of adjusted EBITDA and $2.8B to $3.3B of FCF before growth.

There are clearly tailwinds, and the company seems positioned to capture part of this new reality. However, I still need to check how much of this demand actually ends up in cash, and if that is the case, whether we are still on time to get into a company that is indirectly benefiting from the new AI revolution.

Valuation, Leverage, and 12-Month Price Target

As we discussed, I am going to use the midpoint of EBITDA, assuming for this next FY26 around $5.58B, and FCF before growth of $3.05B. Just to clarify the term, FCF before growth refers to the portion of cash that is generated without assuming expansion, and while it is not a GAAP metric, it is a metric used by NRG itself and the market’s main reference point, which is why I use it.

So, which items impact these metrics? I start with the fact that, historically, I see in NRG a significant volatility in OCF driven by working capital due to collateral requirements linked to energy hedges, so when wholesale prices move abruptly higher or lower, working capital ends up absorbing these timing mismatches on a temporary basis. Therefore, to assume an FCF that can be defended in a reasonable way for this FY26, we need an environment where volatility does not exist or can be managed, together with a hedging structure that does not put pressure on the company’s balance sheet.

Leverage also matters, as after the integration of LS Power, net debt sits around the $11B range, so with an estimated EBITDA of about $5.58B, we are looking at a net debt/EBITDA ratio close to 2x. In my view, that is not an alarming level, but it is meaningful enough that a relevant financing cost can show up, and it has to be monitored and quantified. In fact, if the average cost of debt is around 6%, we are talking about roughly $660M per year going straight into interest expense.

Finally, I also look at CapEx, because if guidance is framed in terms of FCF before growth, then maintenance CapEx is already deducted, leaving out expansionary CapEx. Based on what can be seen in the financial statements, in recent years investment levels have been above the pre-COVID period, so with the power system increasing demand year after year, I see no reason to assume CapEx will go back to prior lows. Therefore, my first scenario would look like this:

My next step is to determine what multiple can be assigned, taking cash generation into account, but also the role NRG plays in the current setup, as that also needs to be quantified, even if it is more intangible. Comparable businesses have traded in a 10x to 13x FCF range depending on the energy cycle, so that is the first reference I use to check positioning and determine whether the stock could suggest undervaluation or overvaluation at those levels. Applying that range, we get:

At current market prices, considering the stock is trading around the $173 per share area and looking at the recent price appreciation and the current market setup, I think there is still room to the upside. That said, in the next report we will need to confirm that the company remains stable in terms of margins, working capital control, and the consistency of converting EBITDA into cash. If that consistency is proven, in my view the multiple should trade toward the upper end of the range, with limited downside, as it would be supported by controlled cash generation.

The Real Risks Behind NRG’s Cash Flow

My main scenario assumes FCF before growth of around $3.05B, so the key risk revolves around the impacts it could take, meaning under what conditions NRG is able to maintain stability in OCF. The company uses financial hedges to lock in electricity and gas prices, but when those positions generate collateral requirements, as we discussed in the previous section, they can create OCF impacts of hundreds of millions of dollars.

If, for example, in 2026 we see abrupt price moves, and a mismatch in the $300M to $500M range shows up in working capital, we would see FCF reduced to a range closer to $2.5B, and that would automatically push us toward the upper end of the valuation range, i.e., 13x.

But it can also happen that, with EBITDA at $5.58B, which is the scenario I am working with, and considering net debt, if NRG saw a margin compression of, for example, 5%, we would be talking about a decline of around -$275M in EBITDA.

After the interest expense we quantified at around $660M and maintenance CapEx, it would be reasonable to justify FCF per share levels around $14.5, or in other words, $174 per share if we apply a conservative 12x multiple.

So the scenario I will be watching is whether these risks begin to surface or whether we start to see an adjustment in the price. In that case, it could be reasonable to set multiples of 11x or 10x, as the market could be pricing in higher financial volatility and a more limited ability to sustain spreads.

Positioned to Monetize the AI Power Boom

My takeaway, after putting together all the information available, is to rate NRG a Buy.

To me, U.S. power demand is a reality that shows up in TWh, in interconnection queues, and in the CapEx revisions we are seeing today. In that setup, NRG is a very interesting piece on the board, as it is not a traditional regulated utility, and it operates more like an operator that can convert the volatility that shows up in the market into margin, as long as it keeps executing the way it has so far.

My base case points to FCF before growth of around $3.05B, and if I consider a balance sheet that is running at roughly 2x net debt/EBITDA, I think the current price of $173 at the time of writing suggests the market is not fully pricing the context in which NRG is operating.

So, in my view, there is an opportunity here. However, as always, we will need to stay focused on the next results to confirm the consistency of converting EBITDA into cash and to monitor how working capital moves.

Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Acorn Fund (ACRNX) returned 1.24% during the fourth quarter.
  • The fund’s benchmark, the Russell 2500 Growth Index, returned 0.33% for the same period.
  • For monthly performance information, please check online at columbiathreadneedleus.com.

Overview and performance drivers

The fourth quarter saw the Russell 2500 Growth Index finish essentially flat. While lower interest rates and declining headline inflation continued to support markets into year end, a significant Santa Claus rally failed to materialize, and value outperformed growth. Gains were strongest within the health care, real estate and communication services sectors, including a sustained risk-on rally in biotechnology stocks. Conversely, real estate, utilities and information technology posted declines, in the last case, likely due to shorter-term mean reversion and profit-taking.

Our philosophy and process, including a disciplined valuation framework, started to show signs of life following a difficult few quarters of trying to keep pace in a market fueled by more speculative stocks. From a sector perspective, strong stock selection in information technology and consumer discretionary contributed positively to relative returns, while positioning in health care detracted from performance. Our strategy typically underweights biotechnology and pharmaceuticals, choosing to overweight the life sciences “picks and shovels” of health care innovation. However, biopharma’s waves of risk-on momentum and binary clinical trial outcomes can sometimes result in shorter-term headwinds.

Overall, stock selection rather than sector allocation was the primary driver of the fund's outperformance during the quarter. Our leading contributors were primarily within health care and information technology, while our detractors were largely idiosyncratic and spread across a few different industries.

Leading contributors

  • Exact Sciences is a provider of non-invasive cancer screening and therapy selection tests with its leading stool-based test Cologuard. A long-time holding, the stock outperformed meaningfully during the quarter, following very strong and better-than-expected third-quarter results posted in early November, as well as the announced acquisition by Abbott Laboratories just a few weeks later. Abbott is acquiring Exact Sciences for $105 per share, representing an equity value of approximately $21 billion with an expected close in the second quarter of 2026. We exited Exact Sciences in the weeks following the announcement, but view the combined company as well-positioned to be a leader in global cancer screening and precision oncology diagnostics.
  • Coherent shares rose sharply in the quarter, and the stock remains a compelling holding, strategically positioned as a leader in optical components that enable high-speed datacenter interconnects essential for artificial intelligence (AI) workloads. The company has multiple growth catalysts, including datacenter capacity additions driven by AI initiatives, telecom recovery following inventory digestion, new product commercialization in optical switches and industrial market stabilization. Moreover, we believe the company continues to trade at a reasonable multiple relative to its growth trajectory. Coherent has significant margin expansion opportunities through improving product mix, operational leverage and acquisition synergies.
  • Celestica designs and manufactures essential hardware that powers AI datacenters, working directly with the biggest tech companies (Google, Meta, Amazon) to build custom solutions for specific AI needs, including high-speed switches that connect thousands of AI servers, along with specialized servers and high-capacity storage systems. Over the quarter, Celestica continued to land deals, including one with OpenAI, while further strengthening its position with hyperscaler customers. We continue to see attractive risk/reward in the stock, and it remains a large technology position.

Varonis Systems (VRNS) -0.26

Leading detractors

  • Parsons is a standout in the defense and infrastructure space, with a strong focus on cybersecurity, intelligence and critical infrastructure protection. With a 75-year history, Parsons has built an increasingly differentiated high-end talent base in the control, communications, computers, cyber, intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance spaces as well as in the cyber domain, which complement its legacy positions in missile defense and traditional federal services. It is also the leading player in the Middle East market (20% of revenues). Parsons' stock sold off during the quarter following the news that the company did not win a significant FAA modernization contract. While disappointing, management emphasized that this does not impact the company’s existing FAA business, and they expect to receive significant incremental work on current contracts. We view the market reaction as excessive, given Parsons' strong positioning in growing defense areas like cybersecurity and critical infrastructure, where spending continues to increase.
  • e.l.f. Beauty is a cosmetics and skincare company offering strong value under the e.l.f brand, effective skincare under Naturium and, more recently, premium cosmetics under Rhode. The company provided initial annual earnings guidance well below expectations. The majority of the disappointment was due to the fact the company halted shipments to certain retailers in the quarter due to their delays in implementing tariff-driven August 1 price increases. However, sell-through to end consumers remained strong, and the company continued to take share in the category. We view the sales pause as a smart move to protect the brand and retailer relationships and continue to see potential for significant global expansion through increasing shelf space at existing and new retailers, as well as adding new products (especially for Rhode). The stock has rebounded sharply so far in 2026.
  • Janus International Group is a leading global manufacturer and supplier of turn-key self-storage and commercial and industrial building solutions. The company holds a dominant market position in the self-storage industry, with nearly 70% market share and has been expanding its presence internationally. Janus generates strong free cash flow and maintains solid return metrics driven by its competitive moat in the self-storage market. The stock experienced a significant sell-off due to weaker-than-expected results, driven by some project delays in its self-storage segment related to efforts to implement more uniform branding across subsidiaries. Despite these near-term headwinds, Janus remains attractive, with stable backlogs and construction-ready sites that could begin development within a quarter or two once customers decide to move forward. The company appears well-positioned for recovery once the current macroeconomic uncertainties subside.

Outlook

We found 2025 challenging as small- and mid-cap investors. Given our philosophy and process, we struggled with the dominance of themes and memes driving up valuations, partially due to elevated retail investor participation. In addition, the macroeconomic environment has been choppy and confusing, with changing conditions impacting consumer and corporate sentiment and creating rolling recessions in various pockets of the economy. That said, entering 2026 we believe the current backdrop represents an attractive setup for multi-year outperformance for smaller caps. The combination of more realistic valuations, particularly relative to large caps, easing monetary headwinds and re-emerging merger and acquisition activity should all benefit the group.

As part of our risk management process, we are aware of the themes and factors driving the market, but our investment philosophy and process remain consistent. We remain growth investors focused on holding innovative and high-quality, small- and mid-cap companies trading at discounts to their intrinsic value. We refer to these companies as "classification misfits," as the stock price reflects a misunderstanding of the business model or economic value creation. While this can take some time to play out, we believe market volatility can provide this approach with attractive opportunities.

In recent commentaries, we have discussed our substantial holdings in the AI and defense areas. We remain overweight aerospace and defense, seeing several companies poised to grow faster than anticipated over the long term. In terms of AI, we are underweight the information technology sector, given lower visibility and faster changes to the winners and losers. However, we believe we are actually overweight the sector in broader terms, given our exposure to picks and shovels industrials stocks, which are agnostic to more frequent, incremental technology shifts as AI matures.

Following multiple years of underperformance post COVID, health care finally showed some signs of life in 2025 and was the best-performing sector over the second half. The current period (2024–2027) marks the peak for the U.S. population aging into Medicare, and by 2030, the entire Baby Boomer generation will be over 65. Over the next two decades, these individuals will drive a massive shift and increase in health care demand.

Life sciences diagnostics is an area we’re watching closely with investments in a few companies exposed to the broader themes of longevity and precision medicine. Masimo, a global med-tech that develops noninvasive patient-monitoring technologies, and Glaukos, a leading ophthalmic med-tech focused on chronic eye diseases, are two large holdings that benefit from aging demographics. Both have idiosyncratic drivers of growth and exciting product cycles over the next few years. Overall, we believe the recent improvement in biopharma funding and sentiment will continue to work its way through the broader health care ecosystem in 2026, providing an attractive multi-year growth outlook for a sector that is relatively insulated from broader macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty.

In sum, the fund is positioned with a below-benchmark risk profile, which has hurt in the recent environment, as higher volatility stocks have outperformed. However, we believe our positioning is prudent and positions the fund to outperform as the “animal spirits” revert to their historical mean. We are sticking to our process of identifying acorns that we can hold until they are mighty oak trees. We believe the fund is well-positioned as we seek to achieve our goal of outperforming on a rolling three-year basis, while taking less risk than the benchmark.

data.title: Globalstar: This Price Level Has No Margin Of Safety
data.description:
Thesis review

Right after the publication of my article on Globalstar (GSAT), SpaceX (SPACE) announced the acquisition of EchoStar (SATS) spectrum assets for $17 billion. This deal raised eyebrows for most investors, and we started seeing rumors spreading that the next acquisition target could be GSAT. This rumor resulted in GSAT rallying by more than 19% in a single day. To date, the stock price has risen by almost 100% since my sell rating. Clearly this article didn't age well and got me thinking hard about what I could have missed in my initial analysis.

My main concern about GSAT was that it sources the majority of its revenue from Apple, and given that we are seeing an increasing number of SAT companies aggressively pursuing the D2D market, this could result in Apple switching to a better provider, likely SpaceX, which could cut GSAT revenue by more than 60%. This level of risk doesn't justify the premium valuation GSAT is currently trading at. I have noted down a few rebuttals to my bearish take, and they have compelled me to rerate GSAT from a sell to a hold.

The first rebuttal to my bearish take is that even though GSAT has a customer concentration risk, the partnership with Apple is very pivotal to both entities, and given the scale of Apple's operation and the amount of free cash it generates in a given year, the reimbursement obligations are a tiny drop, and Apple would rather expand the base of its satellite service providers than switch from one to another. The only thing that could put the contract agreement at risk of termination is if GSAT fails to provide a better service to Apple after it has launched replacement satellites and fully upgraded and expanded its MSS infrastructure. These are medium- to long-term milestones, and in the meantime, GSAT will continue to enjoy cash injections from Apple.

The second rebuttal is that, suppose the contract gets terminated, this will definitely impact GSAT's day-to-day business operations, but the spectrum assets GSAT has will remain attractive to other partners and competitors. Either GSAT will be able to secure another agreement of equal value from other potential partners (mobile network operators like AT&T), or SpaceX could acquire its spectrum assets. I'm highly skeptical of SpaceX acquiring GSAT right now that the Apple agreement is still in place; firstly, it puts Apple in a vulnerable position of relying on one major supplier for its satellite services. I don't think Apple's management would be happy with that, so my estimate is that reality continues to be the same between GSAT and Apple in the medium term. So for further analysis, it's more prudent to look at GSAT's operational fundamentals like revenue growth, margins, and free cash flow than try to estimate the value of its spectrum assets.

Financial Analysis

When we look at the period between 2016 and 2022, we can see that GSAT was operating at thin cash flow margins, had massive debt on its balance sheet that it was actively reducing, and was running on low cash and low capital expenditure needs. Operating cash flow started ticking up in 2022, and the firm began increasing its debt balance again. After the announcement of the partnership, we saw operating cash flow, CAPEX, and cash reserves hitting all-time highs, illustrating the capital accretion value of this deal. Since September of 2022, when the agreement was announced, operating cash flow and CAPEX have increased by more than 1000%, from $56.42 million and $39.21 million to $786.42 million and $629.70 million, respectively. In the same period, long-term debt increased 85%, from $262.18 million to $485.08 million, and cash increased by more than 2000%, from $14.75 million to $342.29 million. This amount of cash influx is a pivotal change for this firm, as it allows it to further reinvest in more satellite launches and ground infrastructure build-out. Because the cash from the Apple deal comes in the form of cost reimbursement for capital expenditure, it makes sense that the company would raise more debt to finance its CAPEX and then later use the reimbursement funds to pay interest expenses. YoY GSAT interest expenses have increased 114% to $29.66 million on a trailing basis, which is in line with the debt increase seen on the company's balance sheet.

Now it's worth mentioning that these capital inflows come as deferred revenue that will later be recognized as actual revenue over the extended period of time. During the Covid times, GSAT witnessed revenue declines with a YoY growth rate of -8% in Q1 of 2021, but this trend immediately inflected in 2022, and we saw growth rates breaking the 20% and 40% levels between 2022 and 2024. The growth rate has since moderated and to date sits at 8.54%. From Q3 of 2022 to Q3 of 2025, GSAT revenue rose 85%, from $141.67 million to $262.2 million, illustrating value accretion since the deal happened. Gross and EBIT margins also expanded from 54.90% and -42.92% to 65.43% and 4.09% in the same period, respectively. FCF margins have been fluctuating but recently turned positive; this is one metric I am slightly concerned about given that GSAT is not an asset-light business; therefore, it's difficult to estimate how much CAPEX would need to be invested and for how long until the firm can finally return capital to shareholders on a consistent basis.

When we compare GSAT's margins to its sector and industry peers, we can see that the FCF margin massively underperforms peers (-173% vs. the sector median of 10%), and when we look at EBIT margins, there's a bifurcation of peers that earn margins ranging between -4% and 10% and peers that earn margins ranging between 14% and 30%, resulting in a median margin of 11.69%. GSAT falls among the peers that earn a lower EBIT margin (4.09%). Overall, GSAT got rated an F for the profitability factor.

EBITDA margins do paint a better profitability picture for GSAT relative to peers, with EBITDA margins of 38.23% vs. the sector margin of 19.67%, and the firm has outperformed most of its peers in topline growth in the past 5 years. Overall, Iridium (IRDM) surpasses GSAT in most profitability metrics and has the highest EBITDA margin of all its peers, but because topline growth is lagging, the stock price has underperformed GSAT, and this is reflected by a B+ momentum rating for IRDM vs. an A+ rating for GSAT.

GSAT's topline growth has mostly come from one segment of its business, wholesale services, with roughly 60% of overall revenue coming from the Apple deal. I expect this partnership to continue being the main driver of topline growth, as it gives GSAT pricing power without impacting Apple's bottom line. Since Q3 of 2022, wholesale revenue has grown 489%, from $27.59 million to $162.59 million. To give a perspective of this massive growth, the government segment grew by 30%, commercial IoT grew by 42.62%, and SPOT revenue declined by 18.18% in the same period.

This lands the point home that so much of GSAT's value lies in its partnership with Apple and the 85% constellation of its LEO assets that are making this partnership possible. The company is using some of these funds to increase the value and scale of its terrestrial infrastructure to accelerate the commercialization of XCOM RAN. XCOM RAN is a 5G private network that promises to deliver 4x the network capacity of the standard 5G network, which will be a game changer for companies in high demand for low latency and high bandwidth connections for their automation and robotic needs. It's difficult to estimate the impact of XCOM RAN on GSAT fundamentals, as this segment is pre-revenue; however, management estimates they will likely see 50% EBITDA margins once sales start coming in.

Q3 2025 Earnings results

Q3 Income Statement

Looking at the Q3 earnings results, revenue increased 2% YoY to $73.85 million, beating estimates by $4.91 million. Cost of revenue increased 18% to $25.63 million, resulting in gross profit declining 5% to $48.22 million and landing margins at 65.29% vs. 53.96% from the sector median. Operating expenses decreased by 8%, with the main driver being SBC decreasing by 41%, followed by D&A decreasing by 3%. This led to operating income increasing 8% to $10.16 million. Interest expenses increased 284% to $11.03 million amid massive currency losses, resulting in finance expenses increasing 467% and pretax income decreasing 81% to $2.19 million. Tax expenses decreased by 35%, leading to a net income of $1.09 million and net margins of 1.48%.

9-Month Cash Flow Statement

Looking at the 9-month cash flow statement, operating cash flow increased by 352% YoY to $445.77 million, with $340.16 million coming from deferred revenue. Despite this massive cash influx, the company still realized negative free cash flow of $36.13 million due to CAPEX needs that increased by 395% to $481.89 million. Most of this capital expenditure is in preparation for the launch of replacement satellites that are estimated to hit orbit in the first half of 2026.

Q3 2025 Balance Sheet

The balance sheet remains healthy, with cash increasing 567% to $346.3 million and debt increasing 35% to $485.1 million, and deferred revenue of $672.8 million, which increased non-current liabilities by 303% to $1.6 billion.

Valuation

Investors love a company that is stacking more cash in its balance sheet, especially when it operates in a capital-intensive industry like this one; this reduces bankruptcy and insolvency risks and gives it leverage to fund growth. Which explains the premium valuation. GSAT's sales multiple trades at a massive premium relative to peers, 29 vs. the sector median of 1.9, while growing YoY revenue at a similar rate. As a result, the quant system has graded GSAT an F for the valuation factor, which I am in favor of.

But when we look at the cash flow multiple, the stock trades fairly with peers at 9.69 vs. the sector multiple of 7.36. This means investors are valuing GSAT for its ability to generate cash. I personally don't think this is the right metric to gauge with, as most of this cash is from deferred revenue and doesn't speak to the company's ability to increase the value of its product and services.

Risks

GSAT is about to release its Q4 results next week. The main risk I see here that could potentially lead to a price drop is if the company delays the launch of replacement satellites. This is likely to happen as supply chain issues continue to be persistent at the margins, and given that the firm outsources the manufacturing and the launching of satellites, most of these factors are outside its control.

Conclusion

Addressing the main concern I had for GSAT about customer concentration risk, I have come to the conclusion that this risk is not as severe as I initially thought, rebutting the bearish take I had on this name. I still think GSAT is overpriced relative to peers, and at these price levels, I don't see any further upside unless we start seeing revenue from the commercialization of XCOM RAN. This is still in the speculative phase, and management stated it doesn't expect to realize XCOM RAN revenue in the fiscal year of 2025. Hence, I urge investors to hold and wait for earnings results for better clearance going into 2026.

data.title: Dollarama: Dollar Store Trading For Benjamin's - Sell
data.description:
Intro

Dollarama Inc. (DOL:CA) (DLMAF) is a Canadian dollar-store operator and practically the counterpart to Dollar General Corporation (DG), despite both not competing directly in most parts of their business activities. While DG is primarily US-focused, DOL has its roots in Canada. However, both did not too long ago enter Mexico, where they for the first time set foot on the same ground.

Unlike DG or Dollar Tree, Inc. (DLTR), DOL has not experienced a sharp correction between 2023 and 2025, when the US-based firms had to cope with growth challenges, margin pressure, theft, etc.

To the contrary, DOL continues to expand without interruptions. Its stock seems to know only one way, namely up.

The business is strongly growing, don’t get me wrong. I have been watching this name for some years now. But I guess that we have reached a level where the share price has run ahead of fundamentals, trading at an unhealthy premium. I rate DOL a sell due to an elevated risk profile.

Strong execution, but at a hefty price tag

Unlike DG and DLTR, Dollarama is still family-controlled and run. While this is no guarantee for outperformance, I like to see long-term-focused family and/or founders at the steering wheel. Ideally, fully aligned with external shareholders, as is the case here.

Neil Rossy owns more than 5.3 million shares, or the equivalent of around 2% of the company, which has a market cap of CAD 52 billion. So, this is not changing money we are talking about.

Turning to the company, Dollarama has been doing its core business in Canada, more or less similar to what Dollar General is doing in the US, selling stuff at low prices. In the past, for the symbolic dollar or less, the threshold was increased for certain items as an answer to surging inflation. However, the core idea remains the same — selling primarily non-perishable or slowly perishable food, household stuff, decorative items, as well as seasonal stuff at low prices.

What I like about DOL is that the company has significantly higher margins than Dollar General. Gross margins are around 50% higher.

Even more pronounced is the difference on an operating level. DOL has phenomenal margins for a retailer, of rounded up 25%, while DG sees only a fraction of that. For the sake of completeness, DG is slowly recovering from its trough so that it should manage to get back to previous levels in the high single digits, at the minimum. But it will very likely remain far behind DOL.

One core difference between these two is that DOL is strongly expanding internationally. While DG entered Mexico in 2023, Dollarama has been operating and growing in countries like Peru, Guatemala, Colombia, and El Salvador via its equity stake in Dollarcity. In 2024, the 50% share was upped to 60%, stressing the seriousness of this investment. Since 2025, Dollarama also joined Mexico with its first store, for the first time competing directly with DG in a market.

So far, the vast majority of international growth has come from Colombia and Peru. But Mexico will be interesting to watch as it has a population that is around 15% bigger than that of all the other mentioned Latin American countries combined.

But Dollarama is not only expanding in Spanish-speaking countries next to its door. A few days ago, it closed the acquisition of the leading dollar-store operator in Australia (announced in 2025), The Reject Shop, which has been struggling. Interestingly, DOL paid a takeover premium of 110% (!) for a total of $259 million. Management must be very confident to be able to turn this thing around and inject its own growth formula into The Reject Shop.

For the time being, it is not expected that the Australian business will contribute anything meaningful to overall results. Maybe we'll get some guidance next month with the full-year results, but for now, I would treat this latest add-on as an in-progress operation.

For now, DOL has almost 1,700 stores in Canada, one in Mexico, 113 in Guatemala, 80 in El Salvador, 399 in Colombia, and 91 in Peru.

The 401 stores that were acquired in Australia will add much volume, but management will focus on improving operational efficiencies first. If successful, undoubtedly, the torque can be massive, as I believe they paid a good price. However, The Reject Shop has been a very low-margin business so far, as shown in the next chart. Operating margins in the mid- to low-single digits with a falling trend over the last few years.

Time will tell whether they paid a low price and turned this into a money printer or whether the seemingly low price tag was justified for a troubled entity.

The slide below shows what items DOL is selling. Their offerings are spread among 48% consumables, 39% general merch, and 13% seasonal stuff. Most of all the products, namely 61%, are private-label, offering an explanation for why the company has such an efficient cost apparatus with high margins. Around half of the products are sourced domestically, with the rest coming from abroad.

The next slide tells us a bit about the recent business dynamics. Sales grew at an explosive level with 22.2% in the last quarter. Operating and net earnings rose in the double-digits, even though a bit slower due to higher SG&A expenses.

Dollarama is a fast-growing dollar-store operator. Really fast. This justifies a higher valuation to price future growth accordingly. I will come back to this topic further below.

The last point that is important to notice is that growth is to a big part self-financed. Net debt, as shown below, stands at 2.1x to EBITDA, but this figure includes leases for the stores. So effectively, “real” debt measured as net financial debt is significantly lower.

Further proof of Dollarama being a premium company is that it shines with operational excellence compared to other retailers. Their operating margins and ROI are unheard of.

Long-term, the growth story is fully intact. Store count is trending higher, sales follow suit (also on a comparable basis, not just throw more stores), with the bottom line expanding as well.

The company has been opening on average 66 new stores annually over the last ten years. Current plans foresee expanding store count in Canada to 2,200 entities by 2034, from the current almost 1,700, as shown above. So, this growth story seems to be far from finished.

Internationally, we have obviously much room to grow. Canada is likely the most mature market, but DOL still intends to expand meaningfully.

Its Latin American opportunity is vast. With less than half of the total stores compared to Canada but a six times higher population, the white space is clearly there. Even if only half as successful as in Canada, there’s plenty of opportunity. In Australia, the store count is currently only a quarter compared to Canada. Despite the population being a third lower than in Canada, even their growth through higher store count looks possible, on top of turning The Reject Shop from an ultra-low-margin business into a solid contributor.

With that said, what’s the price tag for this promising growth story, you might ask. This is a question I ask myself when looking at strongly growing companies like DOL.

Unfortunately, the market cap is a hefty CAD 52 billion. Net financial debt without leases is around CAD 2 billion for an enterprise value of around CAD 54 billion. Despite having high margins and generating robust free cash flow, we only come out at CAD 1.5 billion pre-leasing payments and a bit less than CAD 1.4 billion after leasing for FCF.

The EV / FCF is around 40x, or the current FCF yield is below 3%.

This is a too-aggressive valuation for me. Even at current growth rates of above 20% (for sales). At this pace, DOL could double in size around every three years. This means, in 2029, the expected FCF yield would still be just 6%. I would be justifiable even if I knew that DOL keeps on compounding for longer than these three years at this pace.

However, this is a bold assumption. What has worked in the past does not have to work in the future. Respectively, the bigger the company becomes, the harder sustaining the growth rates. In my view, at a minimum, a doubling in operations is already priced in. The problem is, what if growth slows down? We have observed that with Dollar General between 2023 and 2025, where sales growth compressed strongly to low single-digit territory, while at the same time, margins came down.

The stock was likewise valued at a perfect north of 30x multiples. Surprisingly, the market did not like the step on the brakes, sending DG down from $250 to well below $100 within the next quarters, before the recovery set in.

My fear from a risk and reward perspective is that DOL could face a similar outcome if something stops running perfectly. DOL today is even higher valued on a multiple basis than DG was at its high. In my view, it very well deserves a premium compared to DG, as DOL has much higher margins and a clearer growth path. That’s not the issue.

But if something breaks, even if only temporarily, DOL stock could crater brutally. Challenges to master are plenty, starting with the turnaround in Australia that could either flop or just take longer than thought. In Canada, market potential could prove to be lower than thought, and/or consumers could remain cautious due to ongoing cost-of-living challenges. Mexico and the other Latin American countries could also lead to slower-than-expected growth for whatever reason.

My problem is that DOL stock is priced perfectly. I do not like that. At 20x multiples, I would be very fine with the setup.

But not at 40x. Sell, waiting for a better entry point.

To prove that DOL is not entirely immune to falling stock prices, we just need to take a look at the year 2018, where temporary growth slowed down. From high to low, DOL shares got cut in half. From lower absolute multiples, but admittedly also on lower business dynamics.

But today, we have more variables, i.e., more risk factors.

Risks to my Thesis

One clear risk factor for my bearish thesis is that Dollarama just continues execution. If it even overdelivers, for example, with its expansion into Mexico / and or Australia, growth could even accelerate, making the currently rich-looking multiple cheaper in hindsight than I am currently assuming.

If consumer sentiment recovers, or if more consumers switch to Dollarama seeking value, this could boost margins and the bottom line for more robust growth.

Dollarama has a clean balance sheet and is aggressively buying back shares. Even though the torque has fallen now strongly (less shares per buyback), these repurchases could still be supportive for the stock price.

Conclusion

I like Dollarama as a business. Execution is strong, and so are the future growth prospects. However, the stock seems richly valued. Sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Energy Transfer Earnings Reaction: It's Now Fairly Valued (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It is vitally important that investors keep their outlooks, models, and projections regularly updated. There are a lot of variables at play in capital markets, and they often change quickly. This sentiment is true even for a company as stable and consistent as Energy Transfer, which released its Q4 2025 earnings results on February 17.

This article will serve as a follow-up to Energy Transfer: A Great Long-Term Buying Opportunity. Some key elements to my investment thesis then were:

  1. ET’s increasing focus on moving natural gas leaves investors with a very long investment timeframe, even for a fossil fuels company.

  2. ET is the beneficiary of a massive tailwind in the form of ever-increasing demand for natural gas as a result of the electricity requirements of AI data centers.

  3. ET’s manageable debt load, capital expansion plans, and backlog of projects leave the distribution exceedingly safe.

The price for ET’s common units at publication of my initial coverage was $16.49. Since the initial publication, the unit price has climbed to $18.75 at the time of this writing, meaning investors purchasing then have enjoyed a 13.7% price return and a 15.74% total return, including the February 19 distribution of $.335 per unit. If only all of my investment theses panned out so well.

With the information included in the Q4 earnings call, in my view, the market has effectively re-priced ET’s common units to reflect fair value.

Q4 Earnings Call Updates

One of the aspects of ET that I find most promising is the consistency and predictability of the earnings calls and releases. As the business model is primarily an energy transporter, this means that there is very little variance or surprises come earnings time. This also means that the management team spends most of their earnings calls with a forward-looking approach on projects in development, as opposed to digging into financial results that have already happened. This gives me a greater sense of confidence in the valuation model used to calculate intrinsic value.

Operationally, ET had another excellent quarter of execution by setting volume records across all five of the business segments. ET’s earnings are now exceedingly well-balanced across each of these segments.

Regarding forward guidance for 2026, the management team slightly raised the EBITDA outlook from $17.45 to $17.85 billion, but this was isolated to USA Compression’s acquisition of J-W Power and not a result of unanticipated organic growth. The overall tone of the management team in the earnings call was very bullish, and it seems likely that there could be further increases in guidance as a result of some projects coming online earlier than expected. Co-CEO and Director Thomas Long had this update on the much-anticipated Hugh Brinson project:

Organic growth cap ex came in as expected at $5 to $5.5 billion, with some increased clarity on how these investment dollars will be spent. Two-thirds of this growth cap expenditure is allocated towards increasing natural gas capabilities. Most importantly, the diameter of the pipeline of the Desert Southwest project has been upsized from 42” to 48” as announced in December 2025 and is expected to be online in Q4 2029. Additionally, two new Florida-focused projects were added to an already extensive backlog of natural gas projects that were not included in the Q3 2025 earnings report. The Florida Gas Transmission and the South Florida Project are anticipated to be online in 2028 and 2030, respectively. In the Q3 2025 earnings call, the management team announced a long-term agreement with Oracle, and some gas is already being provided with the pipelines necessary to supply this agreement, which is expected to be completed in mid-2026.

With raised guidance, new capital projects announced, and previously announced projects likely being online ahead of schedule, my takeaways from the Q4 earnings call reflect increased operational clarity, reduced execution risk, and increased confidence in the valuation model. The Q4 earnings call is best summarized through this quote from Mr. Thomas Long:

Updated Valuation

When I initially start covering a company, I like to build in multiple layers of conservatism into my valuations because, as the saying goes, there’s no called strikes in this game. Previously, I had an intrinsic value calculation of $17.12 as a result of an 11% required rate of return and a 3% long-term distribution growth rate. This valuation was purely derived from the expected future distributions, with no factoring in capital appreciation.

While it is both financially and emotionally rewarding to see conservative expectations easily met, setting valuations too low can lead to missed opportunities. The first update to the valuation that needs to be made is the distribution increase of .8% distribution increase announced at the end of January from $.3325 per unit to $.335 per unit. Using the same required rate of return of 11% and the long-term distribution growth rate of 3%, this recently announced distribution hike brings the valuation to $17.25.

The management team has increased the distribution by $.0025 every quarter since Q1 2023 and targets 3%–5% long-term distribution growth. Factoring in the raised 2026 guidance and even more projects added to the backlog, in my view, a 3% long-term distribution growth rate is too conservative. Increasing the distribution growth rate to 3.5%, which is still well below the 5-year distribution growth rate of 7.69%, brings the valuation to $18.49. I consider this to be both a reasonable and conservative valuation, and I will consider this to be the base-case scenario.

However, the other piece of the valuation here is the required rate of return. The previous 11% required rate of return was arrived at by adding an extra risk premium on top of the typical 9% market risk due to the debt load and some vulnerability to energy price fluctuations. The price of WTI has increased by about 10% since starting my initial coverage, and natural gas prices have declined by around 20%. However, this is likely just a return to a more normal price as seasonal demand wanes as winter subsides. The price of natural gas is forecasted to rise through 2027. The Q4 2025 earnings report contained no material updates about debt, outside of reiterating the 4.0 - 4.5x EBITDA leverage target. Increased guidance and the completion of new projects should ease at least some concerns about the debt (see: ‘Updated Risks’ below). With some modest improvements to the risk factors of debt and energy prices, a more bullish take on the sensitivity analysis reduces the required rate of return to 10.5%. With this reduced required rate of return, an increased long-term distribution growth rate of 3.5%, and a new current distribution payout of $.335, I have the updated valuation at $21.33 as the bull case.

Updated Risks

As mentioned, one of the chief concerns for ET unit holders is the sustainability of the debt load. Those concerned about the debt load recently got some good news, as ET’s management team issued some new bonds where the funds raised are designated for existing debt refinancing. The financing costs are 4.55% through 2031, 5.35% through 2036, and 6.3% through 2056. Energy Transfer does have notes worth $1 billion due this year, and the borrowing costs on those notes were 6.05% (issued in 2023). So, for 2026, the management team is reducing the cost of servicing the debt by 1.5% on this $1 billion. Reduced interest expense on the debt should free up some net profit margin to more aggressively pay down debt, finance further expansion projects, or increase the distribution.

Final Thoughts

The Q4 2025 earnings release saw ET’s common units decrease in price by .75%, even though the earnings results were overwhelmingly positive. Despite yesterday’s market reaction to the earnings, Energy Transfer’s common units are undoubtedly more reasonably valued than at the time of my initial article at the end of 2025. With a current market price of $18.61 and an updated base case intrinsic value calculation of $18.49, I believe that the units are fairly valued. A reasonable argument could be made in favor of the bull case scenario, which arrives at a buy price below $21.33. Personally, as I did a lot of cost-averaging in the middle of the $16 range, I will be holding for now and waiting for another obvious buying opportunity below $18.00.

The change in my recommendation from ‘strong buy’ to ‘hold’ is entirely due to the unit price increasing much faster than business performance. The market has simply become more rational regarding ET. If 11% is your required rate of return or the bull case is more convincing, buying ET now is still very much a sound investment. Regardless of which version of the sensitivity analysis you find most compelling, this is a well-managed, highly profitable business with exciting growth prospects that is worth keeping on the watch list.

data.title: Franklin High Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The fourth quarter (Q4) of 2025 saw monthly shifts in market sentiment as uncertainties increased in the first part of the quarter. The US federal government went into a shutdown on October 1 as the US Senate was unable to pass a spending bill. This, among other things, delayed or canceled the publication of key economic data leading to a lack of clarity on the economic condition of the United States. The shutdown was lifted in mid-November, and subsequent data showed that economic growth in the United States remained resilient. The US Federal Reserve delivered three consecutive 25-basis-point (bp) cuts in Q4, helping market sentiment to improve in December. Fixed income volatility measures fell toward the end of the quarter, reaching their lowest level since 2021. The US Treasury (UST) yield curve steepened during the quarter as the benchmark 10-year UST yield rose two bps to end the year at 4.17%.
  • Contributors: Security selection in healthcare and packaging.
  • Detractors: Security selection in chemicals and media cable.
  • Outlook: Overall, we remain constructive on high-yield (HY) corporate credit and believe it offers a reasonable loss-adjusted carry but acknowledge that valuations do not offer much upside potential from here.

Performance Review

  • The fund’s security selection contributed to returns led by selection in the healthcare, packaging and finance sectors. In contrast, selection within the chemicals, media cable and wired communication segments of the HY market curbed results.
  • Our industry exposures also helped performance. There were positive contributions from our underweight to technology and retailer sectors as well as an overweight to finance-related issues. Overweight exposure to building and underweight food and beverage names and overweight to the chemical sector curbed quarterly results.

Outlook

  • The high-yield market performed well in 2025. As we head into 2026, corporate fundamentals have been resilient and technicals in the market have been supportive. We expect these trends to persist, with bouts of volatility surrounding the implementation of President Trump’s policies.
  • From a valuation standpoint, spreads remain rich historically, but are appropriate given the low default environment supported by wide open capital markets. The Fed implemented 75 bps of cuts in the second half of the year as the labor market softened and growth slowed but remained positive.
  • We remain focused on industry and company fundamentals, the health of balance sheets, generation and use of free cash flow, the resiliency of credits to slower economic growth, and are cautious on deeply distressed names. The U.S. high-yield default rate (including distressed exchanges) increased in December, but remains well below long-term averages, ending the month at 1.88%.
  • Technicals remain solid with strong demand, inflows and consistent new issues. Yields and sub-par dollar prices remain somewhat attractive, while spreads remain at levels that are fair but not compelling.
  • Risks to our outlook include higher than expected inflation, policy missteps, a more severe slowdown or recession, and/or heightened geopolitical tension.

As of 12/31/2025 the fund’s Advisor Class shares received a 5 star and Class A shares received a 4 star overall Morningstar rating™, measuring risk-adjusted returns against 584, 548 and 445 High Yield Bond funds over the 3-, 5- and 10- year periods, respectively. data.title: What Is HIMS Worth Without GLP-1? Earnings Preview
data.description:
Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (HIMS) is down around 50% year-to-date. And no, that is not a glitch. The stock is now down from every time I covered it, starting with the 50% plunge since I first started covering it back in late March of last year. While most of the aggressive sell-off started this year after a series of unfortunate events that I’ll get to in a second, the stock has been losing momentum since mid-October of last year, when it traded at $62 and never again revisited these levels or even came close. The stock was down around 50% from that level by early January this year, and as you already know, it continued selling off due to legal uncertainties.

The stock is finding a sweet spot at $16, and earnings are coming up, and while I don’t think it could get much worse for HIMS from current levels, I’m downgrading the stock to a hold. Sentiment on HIMS has shifted from aggressive growth to a wait-and-see approach due to legal and regulatory risks, and I recommend investors watch this one from the sidelines until regulatory clarity.

Let’s take a look at the timeline for a second.

A trip down memory lane:

The company reported Q3 2025 back in November, and the stock sold off despite HIMS reporting revenue up 49% year-over-year to $599 million as investors priced in the EPS miss at $0.06 against consensus at $0.09 and gross margins that came in at 74%, down from 79% from a year-ago quarter. The drop that followed was fueled by fears that the FDA would remove GLP-1 drugs from the shortage list, which would block HIMS from selling its cheaper compounded versions. These fears came to life earlier this year, and that’s when HIMS really dropped out of the market’s favor. Fast forward to this month, and things got worse. Here’s the GLP-1 war timeline:

February 21, 2025: The FDA officially removes Semaglutide from the drug shortage list, theoretically ending the legal loophole that allowed HIMS to mass-produce compounded versions.

April–May 2025: HIMS tries to pivot by striking a deal to sell branded Wegovy directly. However, they continue to sell their cheaper compounded version by claiming it is personalized (a different dosage/formulation), which is a gray area of the law.

June 23, 2025: Novo Nordisk (NVO) terminates its partnership with HIMS after only one month, accusing HIMS of "illegal mass compounding" and deceptive marketing. HIMS stock dropped 30% in a single day at the time and never recovered these losses.

January 5, 2026: Novo Nordisk launches the official Wegovy Pill (oral semaglutide) at a price of $149/month.

This is where the administration and the courts stepped in aggressively:

February 5, 2026: HIMS announces its own Compounded GLP-1 Pill for just $49/month, a direct shot at Novo's new $149 pill.

February 5, 2026 (Later that day): FDA Commissioner Marty Makary takes to X to say that the “FDA will take swift action against companies mass-marketing illegal copycat drugs, claiming they are similar to FDA-approved products.” The stock plunged another 10% after the post, and the fact that Novo Nordisk issued a public statement vowing to sue Hims, calling the pill an "inauthentic and untested knockoff."

February 6, 2026: The FDA officially announces its intent to restrict the active ingredients (APIs) used for these compounded drugs.

“Today, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration is announcing its intent to take decisive steps to restrict GLP-1 active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) intended for use in non-FDA-approved compounded drugs that are being mass-marketed by companies—including Hims & Hers and other compounding pharmacies—as similar alternatives to FDA-approved drugs,” said the press release. The Department of Health and Human Services referred HIMS to the Department of Justice for potential violations of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act.

February 7, 2026: Under federal pressure, HIMS cancels the sale of its $49 pill. Below is their post on X.

February 9, 2026: Novo Nordisk files its lawsuit in U.S. District Court, with the charge being patent infringement (specifically US Patent 8,129,343) and the evidence being its lab tests that found impurities of up to 86% in HIMS’ injectable products and 75% in their pills.

Past coverage:

I last covered the stock in late September and gave it a buy. Still, I told investors the following:

FDA scrutiny of compounded GLP-1 marketing and Trump's new tariff regime seem to be giving investors a little bit of uncertainty about what had been the company's fastest-growing revenue stream in GLP-1s. Even if the direct financial hit is minimal today, the stock has seen some volatility ahead regarding this. For investors, I think Hims is a good opportunity to hold a digital health disruptor, but it's not for the faint-hearted; investing in this stock means being comfortable with volatility in the near term and being willing to wait out regulatory overhangs.

I came to understand that I had underestimated the extent of the volatility that would follow the regulatory overhang. Looking back, the core mistake in my original HIMS coverage was not in identifying demand for GLP-1s or the company’s ability to scale distribution, but in underestimating how quickly the regulatory environment could flip to hostile. The compounding loophole was never a durable moat but a temporary regulatory gray zone. Once that gray zone closed, the entire profit pool underpinning the bull case evaporated almost overnight. The FDA’s actions, DOJ involvement, and Novo Nordisk’s litigation fundamentally altered HIMS’ risk profile in a way that no amount of marketing efficiency or subscriber growth could offset in the near term. Under this new regime, the market is repricing the stock and asking: What is HIMS worth without GLP-1?

While a big part of my investment thesis was around the weight-loss drug aspect of the business, the core business is showing healthy growth, though not hyper. Without GLP-1, revenue growth reverts closer to the company’s core trajectory. I think the base business can still compound at a healthy double-digit rate, driven by subscriber growth, category expansion (testosterone therapy, dermatology, mental health), and improved retention, but it no longer justifies growth multiples tied to obesity-drug TAM narratives.

Valuation and what’s next

After the aggressive sell-off, it goes without saying that HIMS is attractive at current levels from a valuation lens. On a sales basis, the stock now trades at 1.6x TTM revenue and 1.6x forward sales, well below the sector median. While EV/Sales and Price/Sales look attractive, EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT remain stretched, which is understandable given the business's transition back to its core telehealth economics and the shift away from one of its highest-margin product lines.

HIMS continues to post sector-leading revenue growth, with 78% year-over-year growth and 46% forward growth, far outpacing the sector median. With everything said, the forward growth profile is now less clean. Long-term EPS growth expectations have deteriorated materially, reflecting uncertainty around how quickly management can replace GLP-1 economics with equally high-quality revenue streams. Stripping GLP-1 out of the narrative, HIMS should be valued as a scaled consumer health subscription platform rather than a structurally advantaged obesity-drug disruptor. At $16, the stock is no longer pricing in GLP-1 upside, and a large portion of the multiple reset has already occurred, which limits near-term downside barring further legal escalation. But upside from current levels is dependent on execution and regulatory clarity. So, while HIMS is cheap, it is cheap for a reason.

Until the company demonstrates that it can rebuild a premium profit pool without GLP-1, valuation upside will remain capped, and the stock is better framed as a watchlist name, not a high-conviction investment. HIMS reports Q4 2025 on February 23rd. The company guided Q4 revenue to $605 million to $625 million, with consensus at $619.19 million, up 28.7% year-over-year, and adjusted EBITDA to $55 million to $65 million. Consensus for EPS is at $0.02 per share, down 81.8% year-over-year.

For full-year 2025, management expects revenue of $2.34 billion to $2.36 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $307 million to $317 million. Longer term, on its last earnings call, management reaffirmed confidence in its growth trajectory and reiterated its 2030 targets of at least $6.5 billion in revenue and $1.3 billion in adjusted EBITDA, stating: “With the immense opportunity in front of us, our conviction remains high in our ability to meet or exceed the 2030 targets we established earlier this year.” With the regulatory unrest now up in the air, I'll be watching whether management remains confident in said guidance. Still, I’m telling investors to stay on the sidelines as the risk-reward at current levels is unfavorable.

data.title: Planet Labs: The Easy Money Is Gone
data.description:
Space is becoming the buzzword for 2026, and there’s good reason to get on board. Last year, global investments in space tech reached a record, surpassing the previous peak set in 2021. About 60% of that spending comes from the U.S., driven by hyper-investment in launch services and defense-related programs, and the Trump administration shows no signs of slowing down. Recently, President Trump signed an executive order “designating space as a core national security and economic priority.” The revived obsession with space is due to its being thrown into the ring with AI and defense: the holy trinity for 2026. Planet Labs (PL) stands out at this intersection.

The company, founded in 2010 by three ex-NASA scientists, focuses on earth observation and, to date, operates the largest fleet of Earth-imaging satellites, providing a gold mine of data. PL went public in late 2021 and hovered in the sub-$10 range until its recent 2025 breakout, as shown in the chart below. The company’s edge and subsequent breakout moment came from its transformation into an AI-driven data company, not only collecting data but also processing data on-orbit using AI. PL has been working with Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) since going public and has partnered with Nvidia (NVDA) since 2024 to integrate Jetson AI modules into its Pelican and Owl satellites. Excitement around PL’s opportunity has been building quickly, with the stock now up 240% over the past year and about 216% over the past six months.

Part of the surge in excitement around PL is due to increased attention to space itself. Simply put, PL isn’t feeling the love alone. Space stocks have been on the rise since early January, driven by the buildup to SpaceX's anticipated IPO, expected as early as June, and reinforced by the company's acquisition of xAI, which brought the combined valuation of the two to $1.25T. Over the past two weeks, however, it seems space is falling out of favor with investors, and the hype is shifting elsewhere following the valuation run-up. PL has pulled back approximately 25% from its high of $30.90 per share, currently hovering around $22 per share. The growing concern is the gap between the stock's current price and its fair value, estimated at $11 per share. The question on everyone's mind is, what to do with PL now?

The chart below shows the surge and pullback in some retail-favorite space stocks over the past six months.

PL is acting like a momentum stock, moving with the broader market tug-of-war between greed and fear. Within the space-related peer group shown above, we believe PL has a differentiated long-term opportunity, driven not just by the sheer volume of earth-imaging data it holds but by the intelligence it has turned this data into. The company hosts an extensive archive of Earth intelligence enabled by owning the largest Earth-imaging satellite fleet worldwide. PL’s core product is a daily scan of the Earth, or basically high-frequency global monitoring. The benefit of this strong daily earth-monitoring foundation is that it positions the company as a go-to for a wide range of new opportunities, from defense-driven demand to AI data center-led demand, including space-based data centers. While satellites traditionally gather raw data, which can be slow and expensive, the incorporation of Nvidia GPUs into the Owl fleet enables AI to identify, classify, and selectively send the intelligence needed. Additionally, PL's R&D partnership with Google to use its TPUs in space to run AI models on satellites has strengthened PL's differentiated edge.

Essentially, PL is using AI at the edge to achieve on-orbit processing. What we like about PL is its use of an “agile aerospace” approach, meaning it launches fleets of small, low-cost satellites that continuously monitor the globe. PL needs less cost to keep a full-scale 360-degree overview. This, combined with analytics that turn this data into intelligence, makes for very attractive economics for PL’s satellite-as-a-service model and the margin expansion it could bring as contract wins multiply. While current fundamentals don't fully justify the premium valuation, we think PL holds an unmatched monopoly in its goldmine of data, which will push the stock higher in the long term, but timing is everything, and we think, at current levels, too many of the near-term positives are still priced into the stock. Hence, we give the stock a Hold rating.

The stock is trading at a forward EV/Sales of 24.96x versus the group average of 2.41x. The price-to-book ratio is also far higher than the group average at 22x versus 4x. PL trades at a premium multiple, even when compared to others in the peer group, such as BlackSky (BKSY), which trades at a forward EV/Sales of 7.58x. The market is treating PL as an AI-driven software platform with a monopoly on daily earth imaging. PL is far from cheap, particularly as the market is pricing in this AI premium, which has intensified since the company’s acquisition of Bedrock Research last year to accelerate its AI solutions.

Near-term positives are priced in?

It was the 3QFY26 print, in December, that put PL's market opportunity in perspective for investors. In the couple of days following the earnings release, the stock surged almost 40%. For the quarter, PL reported revenue of $81M, up 33% Y/Y and 10% Q/Q, and RPO grew an impressive 361% Y/Y to $672M, while backlog grew 216% Y/Y to $734M. Management also raised FY26 guidance to $297M to $301M and guided Q4 revenue to $76M to $80M, versus estimates currently at $78.17M, with margins in the range of 50% to 52%.

The market was uber bullish on the results, driven specifically by a defense tailwind around PL’s business. Defense & Intelligence revenue grew 70% Y/Y and 15% sequentially due to contract wins (including the Luno B program by the National Geospatial Intelligence Agency, a contract renewal with the National Reconnaissance Office, and a renewed U.S. Navy contract). In comparison, Civil Government revenue was pretty flat, up only 1% Y/Y and 15% Q/Q. Commercial revenue was modestly down Y/Y and sequentially. The chart below from the company’s 10Q shows the Defense & Intelligence segment’s momentum.

PL’s Defense & Intelligence segment has been making up an increasingly larger percentage of total revenue since the start of FY26, as shown below. In FY2025, Defense & Intelligence accounted for 48% of total revenue, and it is well on its way to making up +50% of total revenue in FY26, according to our estimates. The company has secured nine-figure, multi-year contracts with the Swedish Armed Forces; a multimillion-dollar deal with the German government; collaborations with Japan’s Sky Perfect JSAT; an enterprise-scale deal with the Slovenian government; and other contracts with the U.S. Navy, NASA, and others over the past 12 months. All these positives have been comfortably priced into the stock, expectations, and its now hefty valuation. The company has seen four upward EPS revisions over the past three months and nine upward revenue revisions versus only three downward EPS revisions.

When it comes to financials, PL is also not profitable yet on a GAAP basis, with the company reporting a net loss of about ~$59M in 3Q26. That said, the company is on the right track. For the quarter, PL achieved positive adjusted EBITDA of about $5.6M, and FY26 is guiding for positive adjusted EBITDA between $6M and $8M. Q4 marked the fourth consecutive quarter of positive adjusted EBITDA. The company is generating positive free cash flow, meaning it’s building the habit of having more cash coming in than going out, which is notable considering this is a pretty high CapEx business. While the positive adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow are leading signals for profitability to come, this too has been priced into the stock post Q3 print.

PL remains very much a work in progress, and its main growth driver (defense) is vulnerable to changes in global defense spending. Current forecasts expect the global space militarization market to exceed $63B this year and defense communication intelligence to reach $23.34B. There is little doubt that PL is well-positioned to benefit from the expanded TAM in both markets. However, if we see a hiccup in defense spending momentum, it could threaten over 60% of the company’s total revenue, given its premium valuation leaves little to no room for slip-ups.

Bottom Line

At current levels, PL has a huge market opportunity on its hands, and simultaneously, its valuation has stretched beyond the fundamentals. There are two narratives forming around PL. The bull case places the stock price target between $26 and $33 per share and attributes upside to the company’s data monopoly; no one else has the vast amount of daily earth imaging that PL has under its belt. On top of that, there is PL’s attractive operating leverage, which allows it to sell data to multiple customers once the satellite is in orbit without extra cost.

On the other hand, the bear case argues for a price target between $11 and $16 per share. The logic behind the bear case is that it takes money to get those satellites up in space, i.e., more CapEx. This also raises the potential dilution risk, which has been put on the radar after the company raised $460M in convertible debt. Even if we set expenses aside, the stock carries a higher risk profile given the concentration of revenue from defense spending. If the ball falls on defense, Commercial and Civil Government revenue won’t be able to fill the gap without a reset of expectations and, in turn, the stock price. Hence, given that near-term catalysts have been comfortably priced into the stock, we initiate PL with a Hold rating and recommend investors stay on the sidelines for 1H26.
